reasoning: The informal sum $\sum 1/(k(\log k)^p)$ is only well-defined starting from $k = 2$ (since $\log 1 = 0$ causes division by zero, and $\log 0$ is undefined). The Lean formalization handles this with an index shift: `(k+2)` and `log(k+2)` ensures all terms are well-defined for $k : \mathbb{N}$ (since $k+2 \geq 2$, so $\log(k+2) > 0$). This encodes exactly $\sum_{n=2}^\infty 1/(n(\log n)^p)$, which is the standard Bertrand series the informal statement refers to. Convergence depends only on the tail, so the index shift doesn't change the mathematical content. The hypothesis $p > 1$ matches directly. This is a faithful encoding of the intended claim.
